coredata.dc:title: Sustainable spent coffee ground carbon dots/polylactic acid fiber green composites with superior damp heat resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure characterization and property testing of CCDPx fibers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD spectrum (a), TEM image (b), FT-IR spectrum (c), XPS spectra (C 1s (d), N 1s (e), O 1s (f)), structural diagram (g) of CCDs powder, the inset in (a) is the photograph of CCDs powder and the inset in (b) is the corresponding high-magnification image of the selected region. Photographs of CCD powder dispersed in five solvents (h) and TGA/DTG curve of CCDs powder (i).

Fluorescence emission spectra of several CCDs ethanol dispersions (0.25 mg/mL- 1.5 mg/mL, λex = 365 nm) (a). UV–vis absorption spectrum, FL excitation & emission spectra(b) and CIE diagram of fluorescence emission spectra under different exciting wavelengths(c) of CCDs ethanol dispersion (0.5 mg/ml). Photographs of handwritten fonts using two black ink(top, containing CCDs nanoparticles & bottom, without CCDs nanoparticles(d) in daylight and(e) under UV light (365 nm). The photograph of the two-dimensional code pattern printed using CCDs ethanol dispersion in the dark room when the 365 nm UV lamp was turned on(f).

(a) Photographs, (b) Statistical diagram for Number-average molecular weight, (c) Complex viscosity curve versus angular frequency (190 °C), (d) Stress-strain curves, (e) X-ray diffraction spectra and (f) Orientation of different CCDPx fibers.

(a) Statistical diagram of Number-molecular weight for different CCDPx fiber before and after soaking in hot-water bath (60 °C, 24h). Stress-strain curves of CCDP0(b) and CCDP5 fiber(c) before and soaking in hot-water bath (60 °C, 24h). Contact angles(d), the secondary heating curve in DSC thermogram(e), and isothermal crystallization curves at 100 °C(f) of different CCDPx fiber.
POM photographs for CCDP0(a), CCDP7(b) and CCDP9(c) obtained at the specified crystallization time when isothermal crystallization temperature was 100 °C.
The schematic diagram of CCDPx fiber manufacture process and the structure mechanism diagram of two different fibers.

Scatter plots of tensile intensity(a) and initial modulus(b) corresponding to CCDP0 and CCDP5 fibers after different washing cycles. Photographs of three nature dye (indigo blue, jasmine yellow, lac red) solutions and the dyed fibers (c). Fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 365 nm) of different CCDPx fiber(d). Photographs of cotton fabric in daylight and under 365 nm UV light when CCDP5 fiber was used as sewing thread(e). The simulation effect of fluorescent CCDP5 fiber decorative clothing under the excitation of UV light at night(f). UV–VIS transmittance spectra of CCDP0 fiber and CCDP5 fiber(g). Simulation diagram of UV resistance experiment(h). Photographs of the banana before and after UV radiation for 8 h when it was covered by three quartz glass plates (Quartz, Q/CCDP0, Q/CCDP5) (i).
DSC data during the secondary heating process of six CCDPx fibers.

head.ce:title: Sustainable spent coffee ground carbon dots/polylactic acid fiber green composites with superior damp heat resistance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Novel Functional Polymeric Materials, Suzhou, 215123, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textile and Clothing Engineering, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Textile and Clothing Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Textile and Clothing Engineering, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, China.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer materials have been widely used in the field of outdoor equipment due to their significant advantages such as lightness, durability, cost-effectiveness and design flexibility [1–3]. As we know, the outdoor fiber materials need certain weather resistance properties against humidity, temperature changes, or UV invasion [3]. Polylactic acid (PLA) fiber is derived from biomass-lactic acid, extracted from sugars and starches, and can ultimately be biodegraded into water and carbon dioxide, fostering a sustainable cycle and increasingly becoming the fiber of choice for outdoor enthusiasts [4–7].However, PLA fiber is difficult to achieve the similar water barrier resistance as that of traditional petroleum-based polyester (PET) fiber. During the preparation, production, transportation, storage and use, the molecular weight and mechanical properties of PLA are prone to degradation at high temperature or humidity, which brings a lot of inconvenience to practical application [8–10].

As the hydrolysis of PLA is concerned, it is a diffusion reaction mechanism. Water permeates into the amorphous region of the polymer matrix firstly, transforming the PLA molecular with long chain into a water-soluble oligomer due to the hydrolysis of ester bonds, which destroys the crystal region furtherly and ultimately degrades the material's mechanical properties [11,12]. These degradation products have a higher density of polar groups compared to the initial polymer, which induces enhanced hydrophilicity, thereby facilitating the degradation reaction [13]. Researchers have focused on two modification aspects, changing the PLA's molecular structure by chemical reaction and changing the aggregation structure of PLA matrix by physical blending. The former involves introducing water-resistant functional groups to PLA or increasing the molecular weight of PLA by cross-linking reaction [14]. Its advantage is that the modification effect is remarkable, the combination of chemical bonds makes the structure more stable, but the more complex process and the larger number of organic reagents have adverse effects. The latter modification method is to add additives or fillers to the PLA matrix, thereby improving the crystallinity of the PLA materials, forming a barrier or protective layer to prevent water molecules from entering the PLA matrix, thereby improving its hydrolysis resistance. There are advantages, such as simplicity and the minimal use of chemicals, which makes it more environmentally friendly and cost-effective. However, the drawback lies in the compatibility issues between the additives or fillers and the PLA matrix, which can affect the material's comprehensive performance.

In the realm of PLA physical blending, fillers such as Carbon black [15], Silicate [16], CNT [17], GO [18] and CDs [19] have been incorporated to enhance the PLA's crystallinity. The key point is that fillers should have better compatibility with the PLA, or fillers were easy to form a single phase during the processing, resulting in heterogeneous structure and damage to the mechanical properties of the material [15–18,20].

On one hand, carbon dot (CD) is a kind of zero-dimensional photoluminescent nano-sized carbon materials, which are widely used in many fields because of their rich surface functional groups, low toxicity and excellent optical properties [21–23]. CDs have demonstrated significant potential in PLA nanocomposite applications. The weathering coal-based carbon dots modified by organic amines has good compatibility with the PLA matrix, where it can improve the interaction in PLA molecules and increase the crystallinity without damaging PLA's mechanical properties to achieve higher thermal stability [19]. Although no research reports have been found on the influence of CDs on melt-spun PLA fiber materials, there are some studies on other CDs modified polymer fibers. Carbon quantum dot (CQD)/polypropylene (PP) nanocomposite fibers were successfully prepared via melt-spinning technology, where the fluorescent properties of CQDs were well preserved within the PP matrix. Moreover, the crystallinity of the fibers increased with the content of small-sized CQDs, and the sample containing 1 wt% CQDs exhibited the highest modulus and tensile strength [24].These innovative strategies offer such valuable reference for the hydrolysis resistance modification of PLA fibers.

On the other hand, the synthesis of carbon dots using coffee grounds as a carbon source might address the issue of waste disposal associated with coffee consumption. This method offers a cost-effective alternative to traditional carbon dot production methods, which often rely on more expensive or unsustainable carbon precursors [25,26]. Coffee grounds contain variety of organic compounds, such as cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, fatty acids, lipids, and polysaccharides, making them be the excellent carbon precursors for the synthesis of CDs [27]. Furthermore, the presence of nitrogen in coffee grounds made the facile preparation of nitrogen-doped CDs through simple hydrothermal carbonization [28]. Here, environmentally friendly coffee grounds carbon dots (CCDs) were synthesized by the solvothermal method and applied to melt-spun PLA fibers as shown in Fig. S1. This application would not only increase the durability and hydrolysis resistance of PLA fibers but also meet the growing demand for environmentally sustainable materials in outdoor applications, contributing to a more circular and green economy.

Spent coffee grounds (SCG) was supplied by Lucky Cup (Soochow, China). PLA under the trade name of 6202 was procured from NatureWorks. Ethanol was purchased from Jiangsu Qiangsheng. Deionized water was made in the laboratory.

CCDs were synthesized by the solvothermal method. The synthesis procedure is as follows: Firstly, the rinsed SCGs were dried at 80 °C for 24h. Secondly, the dried SCGs (2 g) and ethanol (50 mL) were transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 10 h. The resulting dispersion was then centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min to remove aggregated particles and supernatant liquid was further subjected to vacuum filtration with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove fine particles, finally filter liquor was dialyzed in ethanol for one day to remove impurities solvable in the ethanol and then in deionized water for two days to remove impurities solvable in water using dialysis bag (molecular-weight cutoff (MWCO) = 1000 Da). After that, the dialysis dispersion was freeze-dried and the resulted powder was named as CCDs.

CCD/PLA (CCDPx) fibers as shown in Fig. S1 were prepared by melt-spinning and further heat drawing & setting, where x presented CCDs content in composite fiber. In other words, CCDP0 meant that CCDs content was 0 % and CCDP1 meant that CCDs content was 0.1 %, and so on. The as-spun CCDPx fibers were firstly fabricated by the SJZS-20A miniature Twin Screw circulating mixing Extruder (Ruiming Experimental Instrument Co., Ltd., China) at 195 °C. Then, they were heat drawn (65 °C) at certain drawn ratios through Table-top Fiber Drawing Device (Suzhou Cassett Precision Machinery Co., Ltd., China) and finally set in blasting oven (60 °C, 1 h) for heat setting.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were captured with a Talos F200X G2 (Czech Republic). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a D8 Advance (Bruker, Germany) with Cu–Kα radiation at a radiation voltage of 40 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on an ESCALAB 250 Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA) using an Al target. The mechanical properties of the fiber were tested on Z0.5 TH universal testing machine (Zwick, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were recorded using the VERTEX 70+HYPERION 2000 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) analysis was conducted using an Agilent 1260 chromatographic system (Agilent, USA) with the flow rate of 1.0 mL/min and an injection volume of 100 μL, where the PLA samples were dissolved in chloroform (∼2 mg/mL) and filtered. The contact angle (CA) was measured using the SCD-350 Contact Angle Goniometer (SINDIN, China). The fiber's orientation degree was measured using a SCY-Ⅱ Sonic Velocity Orientation Tester (China). The thermal transitions and heat flux related to the material were analyzed using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) TA Instruments DSC250 (USA), where the sample was tested at heating and cooling rates of 10 °C/min in nitrogen atmosphere. The thermal stability and degradation behavior of the materials were analyzed on a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA) TA Instruments TGA55 (USA) at the heating rate of 10 °C/min in nitrogen atmosphere. The rheological properties of the materials were analyzed using the rotational rheometer (DHR) TA Instruments DHR-1 (USA) under Frequency-Sweep mode at 190 °C. UV–Vis absorption spectra of CCDs ethanol dispersions were recorded on a TU-1900 double-beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (China). UV–Vis transmittance spectra of CCDPx fiber were recorded on a UV-3600 double-beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Japan). The fluorescence spectrum was obtained using a Hitachi FL-4600 Spectrophotometer (Japan). Polarizing microscope (POM) was performed on WMJ-9875BD (China).

FT-IR (Fig. 1c) spectrum and XPS (Fig. S2, Fig. 1d–f) spectra are used to analyze the elemental composition, surface functional groups, and chemical bond properties of CCDs. The FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 1c exhibits a broad peak in the range of 3390 cm−1 to 3150 cm−1, which is attributed to weak O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. Additionally, the absorption peak at 3010 cm−1 corresponds to the C-H stretching vibration on the aromatic ring, and the absorption peak at 1654 cm−1 reflects the C=C stretching vibration of the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. These results indicate that CCDs possess the certain aromatic structure, which is one of the common characteristics of carbon dots [31]. The strong absorption peak at 1743 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of C=O, and the peak cluster in the range of 1230 cm−1 to 1093 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C-O. The presence of these peaks suggests that CCDs also contains carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups [32]. Combined with the C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1d) and the O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) of CCDs, it can be observed that CCDs might contain carbonyl, ester and ether bonds. Moreover, the presence of N-H bonds above-mentioned in the CCDs possibly means that there are amino groups [33]. Then the proposed structural diagram of CCDs was figured out in Fig. 1g. CCD's structural characteristics would affect its dispersion in some solvents or polymer matrixes. As shown in Fig. 1h, CCDs exhibit the best dispersion in ethyl acetate but the poorest dispersion in water with some particles even sinking or floating. It's good dispersion in ethyl acetate foreboded that CCDs would have good dispersion in the PLA matrix, and the prediction was proved by the results of Fig. S3.

Furthermore, CCDs in this study need also to have good thermal stability at higher temperature and it should not undergo significant thermal decomposition during the melt spinning process. TGA/DTG curve of CCDs in Fig. 1i is helpful to understand its thermal decomposition characteristic. CCDs could maintain 99.02 % of its mass at the given spinning temperature (195 °C), even 95.00 % at approximately 250 °C. In other words, CCDs could withstand higher temperature and be used in melting spinning of PLA matrix here.

The fluorescence property of carbon dots would affect its application in optical material field. CCDs powder shows almost no fluorescent effect in the solid state as shown in Fig. S4a and Fig. S4b, which might be related to the Aggregation-Caused Quenching (ACQ) effect caused by π-π stacking for the highly conjugated structure of CDs [34].Normally, carbon dots was uniformly dispersed in some solvent or matrix to form stable dispersion system to evaluate its fluorescence properties accurately, where the fluorescence quenching phenomenon caused by particle aggregation can be effectively avoided [35,36]. The Fluorescence (FL) emission behavior (λex- = 365 nm) of several CCDs ethanol dispersions (0.25 mg/mL- 1.5 mg/mL) were determined as depicted in Fig. 2 a. Among them, the CCDs ethanol dispersion (0.5 mg/mL) exhibited the strongest FL emission peak, and then the concentration of the CCDs ethanol dispersion was selected as 0.5 mg/mL in subsequent characterization.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum and FL excitation & emission spectra of CCDs ethanol dispersion (0.5 mg/mL) are summarized in Fig. 2b. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) shows strong absorption in the range of 230 nm–283 nm, which is attributed to the π-π∗ transition resulting from the C=C conjugated system formed by sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in the aromatic carbon core of CCDs [29]. And the frequency band at 330 nm corresponds to the n-π∗ electron transition absorbed by the surface oxygen-containing functional group C=O or by the internal defect state [37]. The spectral characteristics of the CCDs ethanol dispersion are further depicted in its fluorescence emission spectra when excitation wavelength ranged from 310 to 470 nm (Fig. S5b) and the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. 2c). The most effective excitation wavelength was 371 nm where the strongest emission peak located at 456 nm. The redshift phenomenon with the increase of excitation wavelength in the emission spectrum might be related to the distribution of different surface states of CCDs and the slow solvent relaxation around the carbon dots [38]. These factors work together to cause energy loss during the transfer and redistribution of excitation energy inside the carbon dots, which affects the wavelength of emitted light [39].

The unique fluorescence characteristics and environmental friendliness of carbon dots make them have great application potential in anti-counterfeiting materials. The CCDs ink provides a special optical function that can be used to verify the authenticity of objects as Fig. S6a and Fig. S6b shown. Photographs of the handwritten fonts, Soochow, using two different black ink could be found in Fig. 2d (daylight) and Fig. 2e (under 365 nm UV light). The handwriting containing CCDs powder shows bright fluorescence under ultraviolet light. Meanwhile, the two-dimensional code in Fig. 2f shows similar fluorescent effect and can be used in modern application. As demonstrated by the photographs in Fig. S6c, as well as the accompanying Video S1, the two-dimensional code pattern is not visible or recognizable in dark room without UV excitation. However, the pattern would be quickly scanned and identified by a mobile phone when the ultraviolet light was turn on (Fig. S6d). In a word, CCDs could not only enhance the visual effect but also improve the security and functionality of the application, promising a broad market prospect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterization and property testing of CCDPx fibers

ce:section.ce:para: SEM images and fiber sample's photograph of CCDPx fibers as shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. 3a. One can find that the color depth of the fiber gradually increases with the CCDs content in composite fibers. All the fibers exhibited uniformly smooth surface, which would indicate the good dispersibility of the CCDs within the PLA matrix. Fig. 3 b and Table S1 displays the statistical diagram of the average molecular weight for PLA chip and several CCDPx fibers. During the melt spinning process, strong shear force at high temperature might break the molecular chains of PLA, leading to the decrease in its molecular weight. The molecular weight of PLA decreased from 92730 g/mol (PLA chip) before spinning to 67517 g/mol (CCDP0) after spinning. Upon incorporating CCDs, the molecular weight of CCDPx fiber increased and the maximum value reached 77048 g/mol (CCDP7). CCD nanoparticles, with surface functional groups and high specific surface area, can interact with the polymer's molecular chains through hydrogen bonds, influencing molecular weight changes during melt spinning. Namely, CCD nanoparticles in the PLA composite fiber could serve likely as physical crosslinking points or enhancers, thereby stabilizing the structure of the PLA's molecular chains. To further confirm the existence of these interactions, FTIR spectra of six CCDPx fibers was performed. As shown in Fig. S8b, the characteristic stretching vibration peak for -CH3 exhibited one blue shift from 2920 cm−1 (CCDP0 fibers) to 2923 cm−1 (CCDP9 fibers). The result indicates that CCDs altered the local chemical environment of PLA molecular chains and the interaction strength between CCDs and PLA chains enhanced. As the interaction between nanofillers and PLA restricts the mobility of PLA molecular chains, resulting in the increased energy barrier for C-H bond vibration and consequently inducing blue shift phenomenon [40]. Meanwhile, the stretching vibration peak for C=O in PLA backbone demonstrated one red shift from 1750 cm−1 to 1746 cm−1 (Fig. S8c). The phenomenon may originate from hydrogen bonds between PLA's carbonyl groups and surface functional groups (e.g. amino/hydroxyl) of CCDs. As the N-H interaction forms hydrogen bonds, which causes the charge of the C=O bond to separate to other atoms. The double bond of the carbonyl peak is reduced, so the carbonyl peak of PLA moves towards the lower wave number [41,42].

Due to the dependence of viscoelasticity on the molar mass of polymers, rheometers can be used to study the thermal stability of polymers or polymer composites in the molten state by monitoring their dynamic viscoelasticity [43,44]. As shown in the rheological diagram (Fig. 3c), the complex viscosity of six fiber samples remained stable at low frequencies and exhibited the decreasing trend with the angular frequency (>10 rad/s), which is characteristic of shear-thinning behavior in PLA fluids [45]. Indeed, when compared to the control sample (CCDP0), other fiber samples showed obvious increase in viscosity (Fig. 3c) and loss modulus (G ″) (Fig. S9b) at any constant angular frequency, which suggests that CCDs increased PLA melt's flowing resistance. As nanofillers could restrict the mobility of polymer molecular chains and the polymer melt's flow resistance increased, then the viscosity and G ″ would increase [46]. As the storage modulus (G ′) and loss angle (tan δ) of CCDPx samples were concerned, test results can be found in Fig. S9a and Fig. S9c. The G ′ of CCDP0 is higher than that of other CCDPx samples in lower frequency region but less in higher frequency region, which means that the increase trend of G ′ vs angular frequency for CCDP0 is slower than that for other CCDPx samples. Meanwhile, the tan δ of CCDP0 is less than that of other CCDPx samples in lower frequency region but tends to be equal in higher frequency region. As the interaction between polymer molecules and fillers in the lower frequency region could make polymer chain exhibit less fluidity (obvious viscosity). In the higher frequency region, the effect of nano-fillers on viscous response is weaker than that on elastic response, and the response is mainly controlled by the polymer matrix dynamics [47].

Mechanical properties data of the six CCDPx fiber samples could be found in Table S2 and Fig. 3d. The tensile strength and initial modulus of the five composite fibers are all superior to those of the control sample (CCDP0). One can further find that the CCDP0 fiber fails to lift one 500 g weight (Video S2a) and the CCDP5 fiber successfully does (Video S2b). The improvement in mechanical performance of CCDP5 fiber may be attributed to the existence of interactions between CCDs and PLA molecules as above-mentioned discussion in FTIR spectra (Fig. S8). Other PLA composite's mechanical property could also achieve obvious increase owing to the improved interfacial adhesion between Nano-fillers and PLA matrix [48]. In addition, the mechanical behavior of CCDPx fibers are also closely related to their crystal characteristics. As shown in Fig. 3e the crystallinity of the PLA fiber calculated by XRD data, increased from 26.7 % (CCDP0) to 31.3 % (CCDP1), 33.6 % (CCDP3), 33.7 % (CCDP5), and 35.6 % (CCDP7), but then decreased sharply to 32.3 % (CCDP9). This trend indicates that the addition of CCDs can promote the crystallization of PLA fibers when incorporated at appropriate concentrations. However, the tensile strength and modulus of CCDP7 was not the highest but that of CCDP5 was, which might be related to the comprehensive consideration of both crystallinity and orientation degree of the two fibers. The straightening of the orientation of the molecular chain will result in the strengthening of the fiber during rapid stretching, and fiber with the higher orientation might exhibit the more uniform arrangement in the direction of tension, enabling the material to transmit effectively stress during stretching and resulting in higher tensile strength [49]. As depicted in Fig. 3f, CCDP5 fiber has the highest orientation degree (fs = 0.708), hence it exhibits the highest strength and modulus among the six fiber samples. Based on the above characterization and testing, the addition of CCDs is helpful to improve the spinnability of PLA fiber.

Damp heat resistance is very important for the practical application of textiles. Understanding how different fibers behave in hot and humid environments can help us select suitable fiber materials for outdoor fabrics. Here, a series of experiments and characterization analyses were conducted to evaluate the damp heat resistance for different CCDPx fibers.

In the hot-water soaking experiment, fiber samples were immersed in hot water at 60 °C for 24 h firstly, then their damp heat mass loss (△wt%), molecular weight loss (△Mn) and the change in tensile strength & modulus (△σ%, △E%) has been assessed. Fig. S10a and Fig. S10b present the schematic diagram of the experiment and statistical results of mass loss rate of the fibers, respectively. The damp heat mass loss (△wt%) of the control fiber (CCDP0) was 1.53 %, whereas that of PLA composite fiber decreased with the CCD content and reduced significantly to around 0.55 % when CCD content reached or exceed 0.5 wt%. Additionally, Number-molecular weight loss (△Mn) is also an important indicator of damp heat resistance. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Table S1, the Number-molecular weight of all samples decreased, but △Mn for CCDP5 fiber was minimal (only 18.97 %) while that for CCDP0 was 34.07 %. The Stress-strain curves of those two PLA fibers in different state could also be found in Fig. 4b (CCDP0) and Fig. 4c (CCDP5), where the accompanying insets show the knitted cross marks outlined on the cotton fabric using the corresponding fibers. The initial modulus of CCDP0 fiber decreased from 405.1cN/tex to 195.1cN/tex (△E% = 51.8 %), while its tensile strength changed from 20.57cN/tex to 6.67cN/tex (△σ% = 67.6 %). Concurrently, the knitted cross mark of CCDP0 shrinks obviously and the fiber whiten, indicative of internal structural changes or degradation of PLA. In contrast, the initial modulus of CCDP5 fiber decreased from 572.9cN/tex to 543.7cN/tex (△E% = 5.10 %) and its tensile strength reduced from 30.53cN/tex to 24.51cN/tex (△σ% = 19.7 %), but that knitted cross mark of CCDP5 did not change significantly, indicating CCDP5 fiber has better damp heat resistance than CCDP0 fiber. Furthermore, Fig. S11 illustrates the Stress-strain curves of the fibers before and after 60 days of storage. The initial modulus of CCDP0 fiber decreased from 405.1cN/tex to 237.0cN/tex (△E% = 41.5 %), while its tensile strength changed from 20.57cN/tex to 9.06cN/tex (△σ% = 55.9 %). However, the initial modulus of CCDP5 fiber decreased from 572.9cN/tex to 567.5cN/tex (△E% = 0.94 %) and its tensile strength decreased from 30.53cN/tex to 28.99cN/tex (△σ% = 6.27 %). The result further underscore that the incorporation of CCD nanoparticles enhances the stability of PLA molecules, thereby benefiting the durability of PLA fibers for prolonged use in outdoor settings.

Lower crystallinity of the PLA fiber means that it has a higher proportion of amorphous regions, and its structure is looser and easier to penetrate by water. When the water enters the fiber, it will trigger the hydrolysis reaction of ester bond, resulting in the break of molecular chain, and finally destroy the crystalline region and the overall structure of the fiber [50]. Fibers with hydrophobic surfaces tend to resist the absorption of water molecules and exhibit low water permeability, impeding the penetration of water into the material's interior and thereby reducing the likelihood of hydrolysis reactions [51]. The hydrophilic or hydrophobic nature of fiber material could be visually assessed using the contact angle (CA) measurement and the test results of several CCDPx fibers is shown in Fig. 4d. The CA for the control sample (CCDP0) is 97°, which is similar to the values reported for pure PLA material in other literature [52]. Upon the incorporation of CCDs, the CA of CCDP5, CCDP7 and CCDP9 increased to 122°, 123°, and 122°, respectively, indicating a significant enhancement in hydrophobicity. For outdoor-grade PLA fibers, this hydrophobic modification enables them to better preserve their properties under humid conditions, preventing them from degrading and even causing material damage [53].

Crystallization property is one important parameter that affects the damp heat resistance of the fiber [54]. The chemical bonds in the crystalline region are often more stable than those in the non-crystalline region, and the mobility of the crystalline PLA molecular chains is limited. The carbonyl group on the PLA molecular chain will form intermolecular or intramolecular hydrogen bonds with the hydrogen on adjacent molecular chains, affecting the infiltration and diffusion of water molecules into the lattice and reducing their ability to attack the PLA chain [55].

The secondary heating curves in DSC thermogram for six CCDPx fibers are presented in Fig. 4e and the relevant thermal parameters are shown in Table 1 . As the content of CCD increases, the cold crystallization peak (Tc) shifts progressively from the sharp peak (∼109 °C) for CCDP0 to the broader peak (∼121 °C) for CCDP9. Although the melting profiles for all samples exhibited dual endothermic peaks, representing two different kinds of crystal structures, α′ crystal and α crystal, composite fiber with the higher concentration of CCDs displayed more pronounced endothermic peak at lower temperatures (α crystal) and lower endothermic peak at higher temperature (α’ crystal) [56]. This observation is primarily due to the intimate relationship between the melting behavior of crystals and their respective crystallization temperatures. In composite fiber, CCDs may increase the intermolecular forces and hinder the migration of the PLA molecular chain during the second heating process, resulting in the formation of the more robust crystal structure (α crystal) at higher cold crystallization temperature (Tc), which have no significant structural recombination or intermediate phase transition during heating [56,57].

The isothermal crystallization curves of the several CCDPx fibers at different temperatures (90 °C–110 °C) are shown in Fig. S12. At 100 °C, each sample's crystallization rate reaches its peak. Fig. 4f illustrates the isothermal crystallization profiles for these six fibers at 100 °C, one can find that the crystallization duration for CCDP0 was approximately 14 min whereas around 12 min for other five fiber samples, indicating that CCDs could accelerate the crystallization process of the PLA matrix. The isothermal crystallization process of the CCDPx fibers at 100 °C was also visually monitored using a polarizing microscope, as presented in Fig. 5 and Fig. S13. One can see that CCDs can improve the crystallization rate of PLA, also affect the number and size of the final spherulites. For CCDP0 fiber (Fig. S13a), the onset of crystal nucleation occurred at 2′53″, with the formation of initial crystals at 5′31″, culminating in the development of spherulites at 12′32″ and keeping stable after that. These spherulites had an approximate diameter of 59 μm, with a relatively low quantity observed. In the case of CCDP7 fiber (Fig. S13e), nucleation began at 1′44″, numerous grains arose at 3′15″ and crystallization completed at 9′41″ when numerous of smaller spherulites (∼31.5 μm) could be found. But the crystallization rate of PLA composite fiber diminished when the CCDs content rose to 0.9 % (Fig. S13f), where nucleation initiated at 2′17″ and the crystallization process concluded at 10′51″, resulting bigger size spherulite (∼50.5 μm). The reason for that might be related to spherulite aggregation caused by excessive CCDs nanoparticle. In other words, incorporating suitable amount CCDs nanoparticles (≤0.7 %) generally enhanced the crystallization characteristics of PLA fiber, whereas excessive CCDs would result in lower crystallization efficiency and larger-size spherulite. As we know, larger-size spherulite are unfavorable to the mechanical properties of the fiber [58], so the content of CCDs in the composite fiber must be controlled. Considering the results above-mentioned damp heat resistance and the mechanical performance of several composite fibers, CCDP5 fiber here was selected as the ideal sample.

Fig. 6 illustrates the action mechanism of CCDs in the PLA matrix during the process of spinning and following heat treatment, where CCDs function as one kind of heterogeneous nano-nucleating agent. In spinning melt state, the CCDP0 polymer displays high homogeneity, free of any foreign particles or pre-existing crystal structures, and retains a clear, transparent appearance. Upon incorporating CCDs nanoparticles, the potential interactions between CCDs and the PLA matrix led to a modest increase in viscosity of the blending melt with the characteristic brown hue of CCDs. The polymer melt was extruded through the spinneret holes and subjected to rapid cooling within atmospheric environment. The further solidification process of the CCDP0 melt during cooling primarily depends on spontaneous nucleation, where crystal formation occurs at a leisurely pace and the resulted CCDP0 extrudates retain a high degree of transparency. As CCDP5 fiber was concerned, the extrudates containing nucleating agents (CCDs) undergo accelerated crystallization around nucleation sites. The stronger intermolecular interactions between CCDs and the PLA matrix also contribute to the improved morphological stability of the blending extrudates, and the crystalline nature of these materials is easy to detect, as evidenced by their distinct opacity.

Two experiments were conducted to evaluate the feasibility of CCDPx fiber in routine fabric application. Firstly, one fiber washing cycle experiment was designed to verify the fiber's washable resistance, ensuring fibers' reliability in actual use. Fig. S14 is a schematic diagram of the fiber washing experiment. CCDP0 fiber and CCDP5 fiber were soaked in glass bottles filled with water, stirred for 30 min at 80 r/min and for five cycles to simulate the state of washing clothes. Mechanical properties of the two groups of fibers were tested respectively, as shown in Fig. 7 a and b. The tensile intensity of CCDP0 decreased from 20.57cN/tex to 12.46cN/tex (Δσ% = 39.4 %) while its initial modulus changed from 405.1cN/tex to 244.8cN/tex (△E% = 39.6 %) after 5 cycles of washing. However, the tensile intensity of CCDP5 decreased from 30.53cN/tex to 24.35cN/tex (Δσ% = 20.2 %) while its initial modulus changed from 572.9cN/tex to 493.7cN/tex (△E% = 13.8 %) after 5 cycles of washing. The result indicated that the addition of CCD is beneficial to the washing stability of PLA fiber. Since CCDs have a certain coffee color, then whether CCDs will affect the dyeing of PLA fiber or not would be worth exploring if colorful composite fiber was needed in daily life. Secondly, the typical dyeing experiments for CCDP0 fiber and CCDP5 fiber using natural dyes were carried as dyeing process is also an important part of fiber finishing. The diagram of fiber dyeing process is shown in Fig. S15. Three kinds of natural dyes (lac red, jasmine yellow, indigo blue) were used in separate dye bath with the concentration of 1 g/L, and the PH of the dye solution was adjusted to 4.6 by acetic acid and sodium acetate buffer solution. CCDP0 fiber and CCDP5 fiber were added to the dye bath (liquor-to-goods ratio was100:1) at 40 °C, heated to 100 °C at the rate of 1.5 °C/min, with a holding time of 60min, then cooled down to room temperature naturally. After dyeing, fibers were rinse until there was no further dye bleeding. As Fig. 7c shown, there are no obvious difference of dyeing property between CCDP0 fiber and CCDP5 fiber when using those selected dyes (red, yellow, blue). In order to further verify the dyeing effect of two kinds of fibers, the colorimetric value of the fibers before and after dyeing were tested (Table S3) and their visually distinguishable color blocks recognized by colorimeter using its color conversion tool could be found in Fig. S16. One can easily find the difference between CCDP0 fibers and CCDP5 fibers before dyeing as CCDP5 fibers appeared brown. However, the color difference between the two dyed fibers was minimal, indicating that the presence of CCDs had little effect on the dyeing properties of PLA fibers. The above experiments certified that CCDP5 fibers could be used as routine fiber.

The intrinsic fluorescence properties of CCDs enhance the versatility of CCDPx composite fibers. As depicted in Fig. S17, the fiber incorporating CCDs exhibits vivid blue luminescence when exposed to 365 nm ultraviolet light. The corresponding fluorescence emission spectrum presented in Fig. 7d shows that fluorescence intensity of the CCDPx composite fiber increases progressively with the increase of CCDs content. Fig. S18 displays the laser confocal image of the CCDP5 fiber, illustrating bright and evenly distributed fluorescence, indicative of the excellent dispersion of CCDs within the PLA fiber matrix [59]. As exemplified in Fig. 7e, the CCDP5 fiber is suitable for use as sewing thread to make pattern sewing on clothes, such as a heart shape and a star shape, meeting the requirements for applications in the apparel industry. Fig. 7f further shows the application simulation diagram of outdoor clothing decorated with CCDP5 fiber at night. When stimulated by ultraviolet light, CCDP5 fiber's remarkable fluorescence might give it potential in some areas, such as anti-counterfeiting technology, outdoor search and rescue operations.

Most clothing materials, whether natural fibers or synthesized fibers, have limited UV resistance properties, and the clothing materials themselves would be destroyed under irradiation from UV rays [60]. Here, the development of PLA fibers with effective UV resistance property is of considerable importance for outdoor clothing. As shown in Fig. 7g, the UV–VIS transmittance spectra at 254 nm decrease from 25.20 % (CCDP0) to 11.83 % (CCDP5) and from 65.79 % (CCDP0) to 42.96 % (CCDP5) at 365 nm. In order to investigate the feasibility in practical application, another experiment was conducted to compare the UV resistant properties of CCDP0 fiber and CCDP5 fiber as depicted in Fig. 7h. Two fibers were tightly bound to two quartz slides, respectively, and the third quartz plate without fiber was also tested to consider any potential influence from the quartz itself. As shown in Fig. 7i, three above-mentioned quartz plates were covered on fresh banana and then exposed to UV radiation of 365 nm (and 254 nm), respectively. After 8 h of irradiation, the uncovered surface of bananas showed obvious blackening, and the banana surface under the single quartz plate exhibited the similar color change as the uncovered area, indicating that the quartz plate did not introduce any confounding effects into the experiment. On the other hand, the banana surface in the CCDP0 protected area showed a faint yellow color and that in the CCDP5 protected area remained bright yellow, indicating clearly that CCDP5 had effective UV resistance. The experimental results intuitively confirmed that, CCDs obviously hindered the transmission of UV radiation, which might provide a good choice for outdoor UV-proof PLA clothing.

In this research, one kind of fluorescent PLA composite fiber with significantly improved damp heat resistance was successfully prepared. Firstly, CCDs was synthesized using SCG as carbon source, and then combined with PLA by melt spinning technology to produce the final fiber product. In PLA matrix, CCDs acted as a heterogeneous nucleation site, which effectively improved the crystallization behavior of PLA during processing. When CCDP5 fiber was compared with CCDP0 fiber, the fiber's Number-molecular weight loss (△Mn) decreased from 34.07 % to 18.97 % after spinning and Contact Angle increased from 97° to 122°, while the crystallinity rose from 26.7 % to 33.7 % and smaller size spherulites with higher crystallization rate and more numbers appeared. To sum up, CCDs enhances the crystallization characteristics and hydrophobic property of PLA fiber, thereby bolstering its damp heat resistance. In addition, the inherent fluorescence characteristics and ultraviolet absorption capacity of CCDs could offer additional fluorescence display effect and UV resistance performance, which would also be benefit for the application of fibers in outdoor clothing.

Zhaoxuan Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiao Liu: Validation, Data curation. Leyu Dai: Validation, Data curation. Jun Sun: Visualization, Formal analysis. Jian-jun Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Chuanxiang Qin: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Lixing Dai: Supervision, Resources. Guoqiang Chen: Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
